summary: Jeff Atwood argues that developers should not design user interfaces, using 'The Dialog' as a cautionary tale of how developer-created UIs spiral out of control. He describes the common pattern where a simple dialog grows increasingly complex as developers add features without design oversight, eventually becoming unusable but shipping anyway because it's too late to fix. The post warns that developers are blind to the usability problems they create because they become accustomed to their own interfaces. Atwood concludes that good developers recognize their limitations and either copy well-designed interfaces or delegate UI work to design professionals.
key_points: Developer-created UIs often start simple but grow into unusable monstrosities through incremental feature additions
Developers become blind to UI problems because they grow accustomed to their own interfaces over time
The 'Dialog' anti-pattern describes how temporary developer tools accidentally ship in final products
Self-awareness about one's limitations is a mark of a good software developer
When lacking design skills, developers should copy existing good designs rather than creating original bad ones
money_quotes: Deep down inside every software developer, there's a budding graphic designer waiting to get out. And if you let that happen, you're in trouble.
Pretty soon the technical team are so used to seeing 'the dialog' the way it is that they become blind to its strange appearance.
Friends don't let friends produce Developer UI.
Part of being a good software developer is knowing your limits.
Either copy something that's already well designed, or have the good sense to stick to coding and leave the graphic design to the experts.
key_insight: Good software developers recognize that UI design is a distinct skill they likely don't possess, and they either borrow proven designs or collaborate with design experts rather than inflicting their amateur interfaces on users.
